title: Organizing a jumbled messy document
description: This video shows us how to use the HTML Drafting Program to clear up a very messy HTML document. There are several sets of keyboard keys described which are used together to combat against the forces of disorganized writing styles. By using these special keys, we are able to easily manage our HTML documents in a way that is both efficient and fun to do. Many interesting and useful concepts are introduced. Some of the most valuable features of the HTML Drafting Program are described in detail and explained in simple words. This is one of the best tutorials for a beginner who is learning to use the HTML Drafting Program and wishes to use the keyboard.

captions.lineThree: This tutorial is unique, and it begins with a jumbled mess of HTML parts.
What an awful mess of things are here!
How will we ever clean up this horrible mess?
To begin with we will introduce the 'x' key.
Let's see what happens when we use 'x'.
We can change the selected part by clicking with the mouse.
Pressing 'x' removes the part.
Now the part is moved into the XP buffer.
We can move between parts using ',' and '.'.
We can also move things and change the selection with the mouse.
Nothing seems to fix our problem. What can we do?
Now we will use 'shift + x'. An entire color group will be removed.
All of the pink parts have been removed.
I will now use 'shift + x' again until everything is gone.
That didn't help very much did it? Now I don't have any HTML parts at all.
The goal was to find a way to organize the parts, not to remove them all.
To bring all of these parts back from this phantom layer, we can use the 'p' key on the keyboard.
One by one, they start all coming back.
Now it looks like we have returned to our starting point.
We now will use 'shift + x' again to do the same process again.
There is a faster way to do this: We use the 'backspace' key.
The menu can be toggled using 'backtick' or '`' from the keyboard.
Now we will do the same removal by using the 'backspace' key.
The 'backspace' key toggles this feature.
Now let's try holding the 'x' key down. What happens?
Everything is deleted in document order.
Now let's use 'backspace' again.
So, we see that there is a special buffer which holds the parts temporarily.
By pushing backspace, the contents of the buffer are switched with the visible document.
The phantom layer is visible but, it's contents will not become part of the saved HTML file.
When toggling the live document view using 'spacebar' we can still see the contents.
Those faded parts will not become part of the saved HTML document.
The same live document view can be accessed using the 'enter' key. We then must use the 'esc' key to return to HTML drafting.
Let's get all of our parts back using 'backspace'.
The keys which we have discussed are 'x' and 'p'. These have there own special 'XP' buffer.
There is another important buffer that works identically to the 'XP' buffer.
The other buffer is called the 'RU' buffer.
The 'RU' buffer is different because it is not visible.
Now we show removing parts using the 'r' key.
You cannot see where those parts went but, the parts are not completely gone.
To retrieve these parts we must use the 'u' key.
The parts appear to be moved around because, they were removed and restored in document order.
Both the 'XP' and 'RU' buffers work on the principle of last in first out.
It is important to understand the interplay between these buffers.
The last part that has been removed with 'r' will be the first part to be returned using 'u'.
The order of return is exactly the reverse of the order of removal.
One more time the 'backspace' key is demonstrated.
Now for the tricky part. We remove everything with 'r'.
Now all of those parts are in the 'RU' buffer.
To demonstrate connection between the two buffers, we use 'shift + backspace'.
The 'RU' buffer and the 'XP' buffers have traded places!
There is a full interchange between the 'XP' and 'RU' buffers.
Again the parts are returned to the 'RU' buffer.
Using 'u' will now return those parts to the document.
'x' is used again. All parts are removed.
It is clear that this is a valuable feature.
We now move on to the next subject: The 'y' key.
Where did everything go? The part has become isolated.
This gives a nice clean view of only one part.
Pressing 'y' the second time returns everything. So we see that 'y' is a toggle switch.
'y' toggles single part isolation.
Now we try 'shift + y'. This isolates an entire group by color.
There is a strong interplay between these systems.
The next key demonstrated will be the '\' or '|' key.
'\' cycles between the color groups.
Now let's try doing isolation in these other groups.
Single isolation can be done inside of group isolation.
Now we can see that our HTML was not so messy after all!
Let's instead use the mouse. Maybe it's easier sometimes.
So now we can see how to clean things up, or mess things up.
Using 'shift + x' again, we put the parts into the background.
Now we use 'backspace' to flip the parts around.
The visible document is flipped with the phantom layer.
How can we put everything back together again?
To be thorough, we will repeat the process using 'shift + r'.
We now must use 'shift + backspace' to get our parts into the 'XP' buffer.
Next, we use the 'backspace' key to return the parts into the document.
Well done! Tutorial Over...
